coredata.dc:title: Solution plasma method assisted with MOF for the synthesis of Pt@CoOx@N-C composite catalysts with enhanced methanol oxidation performance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zeyu
Song, Kefan
Yang, Bingqian
Qiao, Handan
Yu, Yawei
Wang, Yi
Hu, Xiulan
coredata.dc:description: The key to direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) is the anode catalyst for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) which has good catalytic activity and stability. Pt@CoOx@N-C catalysts were synthesized by compounding Pt nanoparticles and CoOx with nitrogen-doped porous carbon (N-C). Pt nanoparticles were prepared by solution plasma technique. CoOx@N-C are derived from zeolitic-imidazolate-framework-67 (ZIF-67) by heat treatment at 700 °C. For MOR, Pt@CoOx@N-C exhibits an outstanding electrocatalytic performance (mass activity of 2400 mA mgPt −1) and stability (70% remained after 300 cycles) under acidic condition, which owing to the synergistic effects among the Pt nanoparticles, CoOx and nitrogen-doped porous carbon. Pt@CoOx@N-C shows such mass activity superior to that of Pt/C (460 mA mgPt −1) due to the fact that CoO can adsorb –OH in the solution and then assist Pt to oxidize the CO-like intermediates to CO2 which improves the resistance to CO poisoning of Pt nanoparticles. Therefore, solution plasma method assisted with metal-organic frameworks have good development prospects on synthesis of highly efficient electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solution plasma
Zeolitic-imidazolate-framework-67
Methanol oxidation
Composite catalysts

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Pt@ZIF-67 and Pt@CoOx@N-C catalyst

And this work was also supported by Cultivation Program for The Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of Nanjing Tech University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental equipment for synthesis of Pt nanoparticles by plasma sputtering in ethanol.
XRD pattern of Pt@ZIF-67 (a) and Pt@CoOx@N-C (b).
SEM images of the ZIF-67 (a and b), Pt@ZIF-67 (c and d), Pt@CoOx@N-C (e and f).
TEM (a and b) and HRTEM (c) images of the Pt@CoOx@N-C. High angle annular dark-field (HAADF) TEM image and corresponding elemental mapping (d–i) of Pt@CoOx@N-C.
XPS spectra of C 1s (a), N 1s (b), Pt 4f (c) and Co 2p (d) for Pt@CoOx@N-C.
CVs of the 10 th cycle and 300 th cycle of three samples in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 50 mV s−1. Pt@CoOx@N-C (a), Pt-SP (b), commercial Pt/C (c) and column contrast chart (d).
CVs of Pt@CoOx@N-C, Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4.with 1 M CH3OH.
Cyclic voltammetry tests for Pt@CoOx@N-C (a, b), Pt-SP (c, d) and commercial Pt/C (e, f) in 0.5 M H2SO4 with 1 M CH3OH.

The current density relative to cycle number of Pt@CoOx@N-C, Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4 with 1 M CH3OH (a), relative current density relative to the number of cycles Pt@CoOx@N-C, Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C (b). chronoamperometry measurements of three catalysts at a potential of 0.6 V for 5000 s in 0.5 M H2SO4 with 1 M CH3OH (c).
The CO stripping curves of Pt@CoOx@N-C, Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C.
The MOR activity of Pt@CoOx@N-C compared with that of other Pt-based materials in previous literature.

head.ce:title: Solution plasma method assisted with MOF for the synthesis of Pt@CoOx@N-C composite catalysts with enhanced methanol oxidation performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Inorganic Function Composites, Nanjing Tech University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Inorganic Function Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Inorganic Function Composites, Nanjing Tech University, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Puzhu South Road No.30, 211816, Nanjing, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zeyu Liu and Kefan Song are co-first authors of the article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The key to direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) is the anode catalyst for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) which has good catalytic activity and stability. Pt@CoOx@N-C catalysts were synthesized by compounding Pt nanoparticles and CoOx with nitrogen-doped porous carbon (N-C). Pt nanoparticles were prepared by solution plasma technique. CoOx@N-C are derived from zeolitic-imidazolate-framework-67 (ZIF-67) by heat treatment at 700 °C. For MOR, Pt@CoOx@N-C exhibits an outstanding electrocatalytic performance (mass activity of 2400 mA mgPt −1) and stability (70% remained after 300 cycles) under acidic condition, which owing to the synergistic effects among the Pt nanoparticles, CoOx and nitrogen-doped porous carbon. Pt@CoOx@N-C shows such mass activity superior to that of Pt/C (460 mA mgPt −1) due to the fact that CoO can adsorb –OH in the solution and then assist Pt to oxidize the CO-like intermediates to CO2 which improves the resistance to CO poisoning of Pt nanoparticles. Therefore, solution plasma method assisted with metal-organic frameworks have good development prospects on synthesis of highly efficient electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Alternative energy resources which are renewable and environmentally friendly are urgently needed in pace with the development of economy and society. Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) have attracted comprehensive interests due to its low operating condition and high energy conversion efficiency [1–5]. Currently, commercial catalysts for DMFCs are mainly platinum (Pt) with remarkable catalytic activity [6,7]. However, Pt catalysts still have some unavoidable defects, such as its high cost and instability in the catalytic process (the commercial Pt/C catalyst can easily be poisoned by CO-like intermediates that deprave the active sites) [8].

Recently, some support materials like carbon-based materials containing metal oxides have been widely concerned due to their great electrochemical characteristics and relative acidic and alkaline resistance. The platinum-based catalysts containing transition metal oxides such as CoO [9], CeO2 [10–12], SnO2 [13,14] and TiO2 [15,16], can promote the CO-tolerance ability and durability. The metal oxides can also assist Pt release active sites so that mitigate Pt being poisoned by CO-like intermediates. Simultaneously, heteroatom doped carbon can protect metal oxides from accumulation and corrosion during catalysis process [17]. Thus, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) derived materials which can provide both metal oxides and heteroatom doped carbon have been studied for DMFCs. Metal-organic frameworks formed by metal ions and organic ligands have unique porous structure, variable and controllable metal ions. The metal oxides in MOF derivatives can also produce co-catalytic activity, reducing the amount of Pt used in practical applications [18]. Till now, there are many methods to synthesize composite catalysts, such as hydrothermal method, chemical reduction method [19,20]. However, there are some disadvantages of the above methods, it will lead to a decline in catalyst performance. For example, hydrothermal method requires the addition of surfactants. The chemical substances could be left over from the added surfactant on the catalyst and lower its activity. Thus, few studies on the further synthesis of high-efficiency Pt/metal oxide composite catalysts are considered. Solution plasma is a unique method which is applied for synthesis of nanoparticles, which has the characteristics of simple equipment operation, synthesis in an open system, and high synthesis efficiency [21–25].

In this work, Pt@CoOx@N-C catalysts were synthesized by compounding Pt prepared by solution plasma technique with ZIF-67 and follow-up heat treatment. In the traditional synthesis method of Pt-based catalysts, the required Pt nanoparticles are mostly prepared by chloroplatinic acid. The acidity of chloroplatinic acid is strong, and there are certain requirements for the reaction conditions in the synthesis process. In addition, the prepared Pt nanoparticles are very easy to agglomerate. We present a novel and simple method that use Pt wires to directly prepare a uniformly dispersed Pt nanoparticle solution in an open system at room temperature by solution plasma technique, and then prepare the catalyst precursor by compounding with ZIF-67. After compounding, Pt nanoparticles can be uniformly dispersed on the surface of the framework in three dimensions. Electrocatalytic measurements showed that the Pt@CoOx@N-C revealed enhanced electrocatalytic performance (mass activity of 2400 mA mgPt −1) and outstanding stability (70% retained after 300 cycles) for MOR under acidic condition. Compared with previously reported Pt-based catalysts, Pt@CoOx@N-C catalyst synthesized via a simple and novel method exhibited an improved catalytic performance and outstanding anti-poisoning performance for MOR.

The opposite electrodes were Platinum (Pt) metal wires (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.9%), which were 1 mm in diameter. Graphene nanosheets (GNs, NO. XF021, Nanjing XFNANO, INC) was used as conductive support materials. Use Nafion (Shanghai HESEN, 5 wt%) as a binder. 2-Methylimidazole (2-MI, Aladdin) and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (Aladdin) were used to prepare ZIF-67. Commercial Pt/C catalysts (Shanghai HESEN, 20 wt % Pt/C) were served as the contrast sample.

The ZIF-67 was synthesized referred to method in previously reported study [26]. Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.2 M) was dispersed in 20 mL ethanol which marked as solution A. 2-methylimidazole (0.5 M) was also dispersed in 20 mL ethanol which marked as solution B.

The solution A and B are both stirred for 10 min, respectively. Then, the solution B was quickly poured into the solution A until fully stirred. Put the mixed solution at room temperature (25 °C) for 24 h without stirring. Finally, the purple product was collected by centrifugation, and then washed with absolute ethanol for three times (named as ZIF-67).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Pt@ZIF-67 and Pt@CoOx@N-C catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the Pt nanoparticles solution were obtained by solution plasma in ethanol, referring to our previous work [27]. Two opposing platinum wires were placed in ethanol as counter electrodes, and about 0.3 mm was kept between the two electrodes during the solution plasma process. The high voltage pulsed DC power supply (Kurita Co. Ltd., Japan) is used to induce plasma discharge between two electrodes. During the discharge, the surfaces of platinum electrodes were constantly bombarded by the energetic radical particles produced by ethanol in the plasma region (such as •OH, •H). Then accompanying the bombardment, Pt atoms were ejected into the plasma region from their solid electrode pair's tip, the Pt nanoparticles solution was obtained. Based our preliminary results, the mass of Pt is mainly affected by the diameter of discharge electrode and discharge time. We can control the mass of Pt according to the discharge time and the content of Pt in the Pt@ZIF-67 could be determined by mass loss of Pt wires. Experimental equipment for solution plasma method is shown in Fig. 1 . Subsequently, the Pt nanoparticles solution and the previously prepared ZIF-67 were mixed together and kept for a few hours. At last, purple black precipitate (Pt@ZIF-67) was collected by centrifugation, then washed with absolute ethanol for three times, and dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight. The Pt@CoOx@N-C was obtained by annealing Pt@ZIF-67 under N2 atmosphere at 700 °C for 2 h. And Pt nanoparticles (Pt-SP) was obtained by solution plasma to serve as a contrast sample.

The phase and composition of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement which was performed with the scan rate of 10° min−1 under the system of Rigaka Smartlab. Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP-OES/MS, Avio 200) was used to determine the content of each component of the catalyst. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI S-3400 N) was used to evaluate the surface morphology of Pt@CoOx@N-C. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI TF20) was executed to characterize the morphology and microstructure of Pt@CoOx@N-C. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Super-X) was used to study the element composition and distribution of Pt@CoOx@N-C. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) was executed to analyze elements and chemistry valence of catalysts.

Clean and polish the glass carbon electrode which was 3 mm in diameter. After this, 2 mL water was used to dispersed 5 mg Pt@CoOx@N-C composite catalyst. Disperse mixed solution ultrasonically in a suspension containing Nafion (5 wt%, 10 μL) for at least 1 h. Then, a 6 μL ink was dropped on the surface of the electrode and placed to dry naturally. At last, CHI750E was used to conduct all electrochemical measurements under a three-electrode system. Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode, and Pt was used as counter electrodes. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were used to evaluate electrocatalytic performance in 0.5 M H2SO4 with 1 M CH3OH.

The XRD patterns of Pt@ZIF-67 and Pt@CoOx@N-C catalysts are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a shows all diffractions peaks match well the diffraction patterns both of ZIF-67 and Pt (JCPDS No. 04-0802), respectively. The four diffraction peaks which located at 39.8°, 46.2°, 67.4° and 81.3° are similar to the standard spectrum of Pt, and they correspond to crystal planes of (111), (200), (220) and (311), respectively. After annealing at 700 °C under N2 atmosphere for 2 h, ZIF-67 is converted completely into corresponding derivatives as shown in Fig. S1 [28]. That is Co (JCPDS No. 15-0806) and CoO (JCPDS No. 43-1004) were detected in the derivatives of ZIF-67. Fig. 2b shows the diffraction peaks of Pt@CoOx@N-C. The diffraction peaks located around at 36.5° and 42.4° ascribe to the (111) and (200) planes of CoO. The characteristic peaks at 44.2° and 51.5° ascribe to the (111) and (200) crystal planes of Co. The results of ICP-OES analysis determine the content of Pt and Co in the catalyst. (Table S1).

Fig. 3 a and b shows the SEM images of ZIF-67. The ZIF-67 has a particle size of about 1 μm and rhombohedral dodecahedron morphology. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, the Pt@ZIF-67 retains the rhombic dodecahedral morphology of ZIF-67. And some amorphous carbon was observed to adhere to the surface of ZIF-67 because ethanol was easily carbonized during solution plasma process. The size of Pt nanoparticles prepared by solution plasma method is about 2–5 nm as reported in previous study [27], so they cannot be observed by SEM images. Fig. S2 shows the SEM images of products of ZIF-67 after annealing, the surface of CoOx collapses maintains the basic carbon skeleton. Fig. 3e and f shows the SEM images of Pt@CoOx@N-C, the CoOx@N-C maintains the original basic shape of the polyhedron, the surface becomes loose and porous which is beneficial to mass transfer and diffusion after heat treatment.

Fig. 4 a and b shows the TEM image of Pt@CoOx@N-C, which reveal the whole framework and outside structure of catalyst. The HRTEM image in Fig. 4c indicates that Pt nanoparticles (about 3–5 nm) is dispersed on the catalyst surface. As shown in Fig. 4c, the lattice spacing are 0.228 nm, 0.203 nm and 0.212 nm, respectively, correspond to (111) plane of Pt, (111) plane of Co, and (200) plane of CoO. Fig. 4d shows the HAADF scanning TEM image of Pt@CoOx@N-C. EDS mapping images of catalysts are shown in Fig. 4e–i, which demonstrate element distribution of C, N, O, Co, and Pt in the polyhedral skeleton. It can be seen that Pt nanoparticles are evenly distributed, not clustered together.

To analyze elements and chemistry valence of Pt@CoOx@N-C, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was executed. The presence of C, N, Pt, and Co in the materials can be obviously shown in the XPS spectrum.

Fig. 5 a shows the XPS spectra of C 1s. There are Five peaks locating at around 284.6 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.7 eV, 287.8 eV and 288.9 eV, respectively [29]. Among them, the first three peaks correspond to C-C sp2, C-C sp3, and C-N/C-O [30]. The remaining two peaks correspond to CN/CO and –COO, which can prove that the N element doped into the carbon skeleton [31].

The spectrums of N 1s (Fig. 5b) also proves the existence of the N element in the carbon skeleton which can protect metal oxides from erosion and aggregation to obtain long-term catalytic performance and durability.

It can be seen from Fig. 5c that the spectrum of Pt 4f shows characteristic peaks of Pt0 at 71.5 eV and 74.8 eV, and characteristic peaks of Pt2+ at 72.1 eV and 75.3 eV. In the process of preparing Pt nanoparticles by solution plasma, a little Pt2+ will be produced. The binding energy of Pt 4f shows a negative shift (about 0.5 eV) compared with Pt-SP (Fig. S3), which attribute to the interaction between Pt and CoOx [8]. By the interaction between the two, can effectively improve the Pt oxidizing intermediate products, so as to enhance electrocatalytic performance of the catalyst.

As it is shown in Fig. 5d, the weak peaks which correspond to Co0 were located at around 779.8 eV and 795.8 eV, while the strong peaks correspond to Co2+ were located at around 781.2 eV and 796.7 eV, the other two peaks at around 796.0 eV and 803.3 eV were the satellite peaks [32]. Transition metals can often interact with Pt and affect the electronic structure of Pt, reducing the adsorption energy of Pt and the intermediate products formed during the oxidation process [33,34]. Thus the existing of cobalt oxide can protect and release the active sites of Pt on the catalyst and enhance the ability of resisting methanol poisoning [8,35].
The methanol oxidation performances are evaluated by CV tests in the solution of 0.5 M H2SO4. Fig. 6 shows the ECSAs of Pt@CoOx@N-C Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C.

ECSA was calculated on the basis of the following formula:
ECSA =
Q H
0.21 ×
M
P t

According to the formula, the number that charge transferred when the adsorption of H atoms on Pt is recorded as QH. 0.21 (mC cm−2) is the constant that represents the charge needed for oxidizing the monolayer of H on the exterior platinum. And the mass of Pt loaded on the surface of the working electrode is MPt. Respectively, the initial ECSAs of Pt@CoOx@N-C, Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C were 215.6, 65.2 and 109.5 m2 g−1. The initial ESCA of Pt@CoOx@N-C was about three times that of Pt-SP and twice that of commercial Pt/C. As can be seen from Fig. 6, ECSA of Pt@CoOx@N-C still maintained 110 m2 g−1 after 300 cycles, while ECSAs of Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C only maintained 38.1 and 46.2 m2 g−1. ECSA also indirectly reflects the electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst, and reflects the contact area of the active site on catalysts with the electrolyte. It can be seen that Pt@CoOx@N-C will show more excellent catalytic performance under acidic conditions. The ECSAs after 1000 cycles were shown in Fig. S4.

Respectively, Fig. 7 shows that CV tests for Pt@CoOx@N-C, Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C were executed in 0.5 M H2SO4 with 1 M CH3OH. Typical methanol oxidation peaks were all exhibited in CV tests. As shown in Fig. 7, Pt@CoOx@N-C shows a high mass activity (2400 mA mgPt −1) at 0.75 V, which is much higher than catalytic performance of commercial Pt/C (460 mA mgPt −1). Catalysts containing Pt-SP has a higher oxidation peak at about 0.45 V than commercial Pt/C. At this position the peak represents the oxidation of CO intermediate products. This may be ascribed to the small size of Pt nanoparticles prepared by the solution plasma, which is more effective for the oxidation of the intermediate products of MOR. Moreover, the positive shift of oxidation peak may be resulted from the addition of CoO, that reduced the conductivity make the reaction delay.

The higher mass activity of Pt@CoOx@N-C for MOR is owing to the strong metal-metal oxide interactions between CoOx and Pt, and the protection of CoOx@N-C on catalytically active sites. Pt nanoparticles are three-dimensionally dispersed on the CoOx@N-C, which effectively improves the contact between the catalytic active sites and the electrolyte More importantly, the CoOx in CoOx@N-C can promote the decomposition of water to generate and absorb –OH species, thereby binding and oxidizing the intermediate products adsorbed on Pt atoms, releasing active sites [8]. The specific reactions are as follows:
(1) Pt + CH3OH → Pt-COads + 4H+ + 4e−
(2) CoOx + –OH → CoOx-OH
(3) CoOx-OH + Pt-COads → Pt + CoOx + CO2 + H2O

Pt oxidizes methanol in two steps. The first step is to oxidize methanol to COads (reaction (1)), and the second step is to further oxidize the intermediate product to CO2. CoOx promotes Pt to oxidize intermediate products by adsorbing –OH species in the solution. (reaction (2) (3)), so that active sites were released and the anti-poisoning performance is improved [3].

Multiple cyclic voltammetry was used to evaluate cycling stability. As shown in Fig. 8 a, c and e show the later 100 to 300 cycles of the Pt@CoOx@N-C, Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C executed under acidic condition, respectively. Fig. 8b, d and f specifically show their cyclic voltammetry curves from 20 to 100 cycles. All three catalysts reached the peak mass activity around the 50th cycle (2400 mA mgPt −1, 160 mA mgPt −1 and 460 mA mgPt −1). After the catalyst reaches the highest mass activity, the mass activity decreases which may be due to the poisoning of CO intermediate products.
The CVs after 1000 cycles of three samples are shown in Fig. S4.

To visually assess the stability of the three catalysts, Fig. 9 a shows the relation between cycle numbers and the catalyst mass activity. Fig. 9b also shows the relationship between the relative current density of numbers of cycle. After 300 cycles, the Pt@CoOx@N-C can still maintain about 70% of peak mass activity, while Pt-SP and commercial Pt/C can only retain 55% and 56%, respectively. The cyclic voltammetry tests more than 1000 cycles for comparison were shown in Fig. 9a–b and Fig. S5. After 1000 cycles, Pt@CoOx@N-C retains 60% of mass activity, while Pt-SP and Pt/C retain only 50% and 34%,respectively. Although mass activity after 300 cycles were hard to support its stability, it decline only 10% of peak mass activity after 1000 cycles compared with the mass activity after 300 cycles. (equivalent to 85% of the mass activity after 300 cycles). It shows that the Pt@CoOx@N-C catalyst has better cycle stability than Pt-SP and Pt/C. It can be seen from Fig. 9c that the Pt@CoOx@N-C catalyst also maintains good stability under acidic conditions. This is due to the metal oxide's ability to adsorb OH− in the solution, assisting Pt to oxidize the poisoned surface CO-like intermediates to CO2, which release the active sites, and improve the durability and methanol oxidation performance.

CO stripping tests were carried out in the solution of 0.5 M H2SO4 to evaluate the anti-poisoning performance. It can be seen from Fig. 10 that the onset potential of CO stripping of Pt@CoOx@N-C composite catalyst is about 0.420 V, which is more negative than the onset potential of Pt-SP (about 0.516 V) and commercial Pt/C (about 0.542 V). The peak potential of Pt@CoOx@N-C (about 0.586 V) is also more negative than that of Pt-SP (about 0.677 V) and commercial Pt/C (about 0.690 V). Both of two phenomena indicate that CO stripping is more likely to occur on the surface of Pt@CoOx@N-C composite catalyst and Pt@CoOx@N-C has better anti-poisoning performance during MOR. Moreover, Pt-SP has a little negative onset and peak potential compared with commercial Pt/C. The result corresponds to the previous phenomenon that Pt-SP has a higher oxidation peak at about 0.45 V (represents the oxidation of CO intermediate products) than commercial Pt/C.

In order to compare the catalytic performance of Pt@CoOx@N-C, the mass activity reported in other previous literature is listed in Table 1 . It is commonly that, mass activity is used to compare the electrocatalytic performance of each catalyst. Compared with other catalysts, Pt@CoOx@N-C exhibit an outstanding methanol oxidation performance under acidic condition, which is two to three times that of other catalysts in previous reports.

In summary, Pt@CoOx@N-C composite catalyst was synthesized by solution plasma method assisted with MOF. Pt@CoOx@N-C exhibits the high MOR performance with the outstanding mass activity (2400 mA mgPt −1), durability and stability (70% remained after 300 cycles), which can be attributed to the interactions between components. Pt nanoparticles were prepared by solution plasma technique which has small nano size and distribute on the skeleton. CoOx@N-C were derived from ZIF-67 by heat treatment at 700 °C. CoO can adsorb –OH in solution which oxidize the CO-like intermediates to CO2 during MOR to enhance the durability. Therefore, solution plasma method assisted with metal-organic framework have good development prospects on synthesis of highly efficient electrocatalysts.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.51772148) and the Project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). And this work was also supported by Cultivation Program for The Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of Nanjing Tech University.
